ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based processed product — dried shredded poultry (chicken floss)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for chicken floss specifically; A0FKP (processed meat product) used as closest defensible anchor for dried shredded poultry preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken floss as a distinct food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken floss is approximately 15g (about 1 tablespoon), commonly used as a topping or garnish on congee, bread, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Floss — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Floss (cr_id F11070001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on sweetened dried meat products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken floss in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sweetened dried meat products with comparable sugar content (34.88g/100g) and negligible dietary fibre (0g/100g). The high sugar content with zero fibre is expected to produce a medium-to-high glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 15g serving calculated as (65 × 34.88 × 15/100) / 100 = 3.4g. This is a conservative estimate; actual GI may be higher given the sugar composition.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 2510mg/100g — more than 4× the 600mg/100g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid chicken floss or strictly limit intake to 5–10g per meal as a garnish only, as a single 15g serving delivers approximately 377mg sodium, representing over 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 34.88g/100g with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid chicken floss or limit to a very small garnish (5g maximum per meal), as the extremely high sugar content (34.88g/100g) combined with zero dietary fibre creates a high glycaemic load that will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; avoid combining with other high-carbohydrate foods such as bread, rice, or congee.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Extreme sodium (2510mg/100g) combined with high sugar (34.88g/100g) and moderate fat (17.41g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit chicken floss intake, as the combination of very high sodium (2510mg/100g) — which elevates blood pressure and vascular load — and high sugar content promotes endothelial inflammation and atherosclerotic risk; if consumed, limit to 5g per occasion and avoid pairing with other sodium-dense or saturated-fat-rich foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 2510mg/100g promotes tissue swelling; sugar 34.88g/100g with zero fibre promotes systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chicken floss entirely or limit to 5g maximum per meal, as the extreme sodium content worsens RA-related tissue swelling and the high sugar with zero fibre promotes inflammatory mediators that directly exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken floss (鸡肉松, Ji Rou Song; Malay: Serunding Ayam) is a dried, shredded chicken meat product produced through a multi-stage dehydration and seasoning process. Chicken breast or thigh meat is slow-cooked, shredded into fine fibres, then stir-fried with soy sauce, sugar, salt, fish sauce, and sesame oil until completely dry, producing a light, cotton-like, fluffy texture. The flavour profile is distinctly sweet-savoury with a melt-in-the-mouth quality. It is widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and China as a topping for congee, bread, rice, and buns, or eaten as a snack. Nutritionally, chicken floss is energy-dense at 427 kcal/100g with a high protein content (32.69g/100g), but is clinically notable for its extremely high sodium (2510mg/100g) and sugar (34.88g/100g) content with zero dietary fibre — making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease except in very small garnish quantities. The high sodium is derived from soy sauce, fish sauce, and salt used in the seasoning process, while the high sugar is intrinsic to the floss-making technique. Calcium (71mg/100g) and iron (4.45mg/100g) are present in moderate amounts. Chicken floss is classified as ultra-processed and is typically halal-certified in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in chicken floss?
Is chicken floss high in sugar?
What is chicken floss made of?
Chicken floss vs pork floss nutrition
Can hypertensive patients eat chicken floss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly and exactly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11070001, confidence 0.95), which is the designated primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed source.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — chicken floss contains no dietary fibre as the dehydration and seasoning process removes all plant-based fibre components; this is a confirmed zero per HPB FoodData. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for chicken floss not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened dried meat products from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for chicken floss was found in approved sources. Glycaemic load per 15g serving = 3.4g (calculated as GI × carbohydrate_g × serving_g / 10000). FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible analogous anchor for processed dried shredded poultry; no dedicated chicken floss code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
